data.title: BGH: More Downside Risks As Long As Interest Rates Are High
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH), I issued a hold rating due to the fund's high dividend yield that can offset market uncertainty, despite its inconsistent earnings. Since my last coverage, the debt markets have worsened, and the fund has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's overall value proposition and outlook for 2026. Unfortunately, I believe that more downside risks remain, and investors should be cautious here if the debt market continues to deteriorate.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BGH's share price has declined by nearly 1.5%. As the debt markets continue to face headwinds, the fund has also lost its positive momentum from only a few quarters ago. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 12% over the same time frame. Following the pullback in share price, BGH now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 12.5%. Distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, and while this may be enticing to income investors, I am concerned about the overall sustainability of the payouts going forward.

Despite the fund currently trading at a discount to NAV, I believe that conditions can worsen over the next twelve months. If interest rates remain elevated, the fund may struggle to regain positive NAV momentum, and this will drive the share price down further. Furthermore, we still have rising geopolitical tensions with Iran, which can negatively impact the appeal of global funds. So let's start by reviewing the fund's underlying strategy that it implements to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

Income funds have historically offered some protection against the equity markets because of their structures. Instead of relying on the earnings growth of a business, BGH invests in a diverse range of debt-focused securities, like bonds and senior loans, which means that the success of BGH ultimately relies on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. This is typically an easier investment to gauge because management can assess a potential investment through underwriting strategies.

The fund aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into income-producing securities while also spreading capital towards a diverse range of industries. For instance, BGH is most heavily allocated to healthcare and pharmaceuticals at 14.12% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to energy and telecommunications investments, which amounts to 10.39% and 9.76% of assets, respectively. This diversity helps reduce any sort of concentration risks to one specific sector. Furthermore, the fund provides some exposure to global securities. However, the fund is still heavily allocated to U.S.-focused securities at 81.14% of assets. So BGH isn't a dedicated global fund but more of an introductory fund for investors looking for a splash of global exposure.

  • United States: 81.14%
  • United Kingdom: 3.71%
  • France: 2.75%
  • Canada: 1.92%
  • Italy: 1.66%

When looking at the holdings of the fund, we can see that BGH provides exposure to many well-known businesses. For instance, BGH's largest individual position is in Icahn Enterprises (IEP) at 2.94% of assets. This is followed by a few other public entities, including Bausch Health Companies (BHC) and EchoStar (SATS). However, the fund also provides exposure to many private entities, which can be nice for investors looking for a greater level of diversity within their portfolio.

However, it is important to remember that BGH maintains exposure to these positions through income-focused instruments rather than common shares. Therefore, BGH is not structurally built to participate in the upside growth of these holdings over time. This can be demonstrated when measuring the fund's performance over the last decade. BGH's share price has declined by 18%, despite market indices rallying to all-time highs over the last ten years. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 126.7% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Valuation & Outlook Risks

When I previously covered BGH, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 1.11%. Following the pullback in the markets, BGH now trades at a much larger discount to NAV of 7.54%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that BGH's price is now falling back towards the middle of BGH's historical price-to-NAV range. For instance, BGH has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.94% over the last five-year period. Therefore, the larger-than-average discount to NAV pricing may entice some investors to begin accumulating shares. However, I believe that the growing discount is a direct reflection of the challenges that BGH may be facing.

The risks become clear when looking at the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $1.18 per share, while the fund experienced net realized losses of $0.18 per share. Looking back on the prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have substantially declined since the highs of 2021. Although BGH is exposed to floating-rate securities, we can see that net investment income was $1.72 per share back in 2021, which is when interest rates were still at their lowest in response to the pandemic. As interest rates started to trend higher, borrowers were forced to pay higher interest expenses. To avoid this, borrowers may attempt to pay down their loans earlier, which impacts BGH's earnings.

Additionally, the fund's net investment income levels do not support distribution on their own. This means that BGH is very reliant on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings. The only issue is that an income-focused fund that prioritizes loans means that growth potential is fairly limited, especially in a high interest rate environment. This dynamic puts pressure on the NAV of the fund, which is why BGH wasn't capable of getting back to its prior 2021 levels. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $17.88 per share
  • 2022: $14.26 per share
  • 2023: $15.20 per share
  • 2024: $15.80 per share
  • 2025: $15.09 per share

One of the main issues is the current dynamic that higher interest rates have on the fund's strategy. During the Fed's latest meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. Therefore, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range, which puts pressure on existing borrowers. A high cost of debt puts pressure on borrowers, which encourages earlier repayments. However, a higher cost of debt also limits the amount of businesses seeking debt financing, which limits growth opportunities for management to invest in. Therefore, there are two outlooks here:

  1. Interest rates remain unchanged, which means the fund will continue to erode its NAV if new investments are not made. BGH may be able to stay a bit resilient, but growth is very limited.
  2. Interest rates start to trend downward, and growth momentum improves. A lower cost of debt provides relief for existing investments and encourages growth.

Furthermore, the fund's use of leverage increases the risks associated with BGH's strategy. For instance, leverage can amplify downside risks if allocated to positions that don't perform as anticipated. Similarly, a high interest rate environment can shrink the spread between the fund's cost of debt and the yield collected from its securities. BGH's effective leverage amounts to 31.73% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$137.5M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage, and if the debt markets see its sentiment become more negative, this leverage can significantly amplify downside movements.

This becomes more relevant when considering that the majority of BGH's portfolio is allocated to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, credit ratings below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These tend to be borrowers that have less consistent cash flows or are more reliant on debt to support operations. Generally, securities with low credit ratings are more likely to be impacted by defaults. The breakdown by credit ratings is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 1.29%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 97.34%
  • Not Rated: 2.06%

Dividend Isn't Covered Anymore

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1223 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.5%. Assuming the fund pays out a consistent distribution over the next twelve months, we can estimate an annual payout amount of $1.4676 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for the year, it is clear that BGH is now struggling to produce earnings that can support payouts.

Historically the fund has done a great job at generating earnings that exceed distributions, but this dynamic has shifted with a prolonged high interest rate environment. For instance, BGH reported $1.00 per share of earnings for 2025 but paid out $1.71 in total distributions. While this can be the nature of floating-rate income funds, this sort of dynamic cannot be sustained. A momentary moment of weakness is fine, but if this continues for another year, there's a chance that payouts will have to be reduced at some point.

Looking at the payout history over the last few years, BGH has managed to keep distributions within a consistent range. Furthermore, BGH was capable of providing a supplemental distribution over the last three years since earnings were strong and produced excess income. Unfortunately, I do not believe that BGH will be capable of providing a supplemental distribution for 2026 since net investment income has been reduced. With the fund's share price declining on a YTD basis, I also assume that BGH may have already experienced some net realized losses for the current year. Therefore, earnings for 2026 may also be poor if market conditions don't improve.

Lastly, the same annual report confirms that all of the distributions are being paid out from net investment income. Therefore, the income received from BGH would be classified as ordinary income, which has the greatest tax burden for investors. This means that BGH would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that BGH is still a hold at this time. Although the fund trades at a deeper discount to NAV valuation, this is a direct reflection of the weaker earnings and threat of higher interest rates. If interest rates remain elevated, the fund's earnings may not improve in the near term. Furthermore, the fund's primary allocation to securities with poor credit ratings introduces an elevated risk of being impacted by defaults. When you factor in the limited growth opportunities as a result of high interest rates, I believe that BGH has limited upside potential right now. So while there's always a chance that BGH can improve performance if interest rates trend lower through 2026, I believe that investors should consider downside risks going forward. The fund doesn't quite justify a sell yet, so it depends on what performance looks like over the next few quarters.

data.title: Coca-Cola FEMSA: An Irreplicable Logistics Machine At A Fair Price
data.description:
Coca-Cola FEMSA, S.A.B. (KOF) is a case that is very interesting. It has that wide moat in physical distribution, a really high entry barrier that seems impossible to be disrupted. In addition, it has interesting financials, manages to generate shareholder value, and also has catalysts for growth in the coming years.

All of this seems great, especially when you open the valuation tab and see that the P/E is ~16x, which seems reasonable for this type of stock. And it really is a valuation close to fair.

But the problem is that many people look at Coca-Cola Femsa in the "wrong" way. I mean, many look at this valuation as if KOF were a US beverage company or a US consumer staples company. However, I think we should be looking at KOF as a LatAm company. A good company, but with Latin American risks, and that deserves a Latin American valuation.

Coca-Cola FEMSA: A Lot of Positive Points

The amount of positive points for Coca-Cola FEMSA impressed me as I went deeper into the thesis. By its profile and by the financials I saw in the table below, I already knew it was a solid company, because if it weren't, it wouldn't be able to achieve a CAGR of ~7% for the top line in the last 10 years.

Being Brazilian and knowing a bit of the local market, I also knew that the moat was wide. In this type of region, the business that KOF built encompasses several things. KOF's moat is very solid due to the scale and the costs that are necessary to create this, but that is not the only point.

The relationship that the company needs to build with various local stores—literally millions of partners spread across regions that are difficult to access—is very important. It's not just anyone who can do it because it requires scale and logistics, but also because it needs relationships and expertise.

The image below makes this a bit clearer. There are more than 11 thousand trucks, 2 million points of sale, and a footprint that goes from more central regions in Latin America to more remote regions.

As I mentioned, being Brazilian gives me a bit of a notion of this. Illustrating, I live in the state of Minas Gerais, and this context is important because Minas Gerais has more than 800 municipalities. We are talking about literally hundreds of cities ranging from the state capital with millions of inhabitants and a good infrastructure to small interior cities with a few thousand inhabitants and a few bars in the city. KOF reaches these small bars and partners with them. Often, they even own some equipment in these bars. It is irreplicable logistics.

The result is not only a very solid outlook, but it also ends up impacting our perspective on the level of growth. For instance, being so large and with this high expertise, consolidating through acquisitions or new partnerships is an interesting method. There were a large number of acquisitions and partnerships over the years. The playbook is easy to understand. Buy a much smaller regional player, expand the distribution reach, gain expertise, gain the right to sell Coca-Cola in that region, and still make the company much more efficient.

Another cool point about KOF is that they have a platform called Juntos+, basically a place that allows the bar owner to place orders digitally. This increases the relationship, often decreases cost, and also increases upsell/cross-sell, because it ends up suggesting new orders to the customer, and with the amount of data it collects, it ends up being another moat under construction. Think, for example, that a bar owner is making a traditional Coca-Cola order; now KOF can suggest beer with a new partnership and can show that the sale of energy drinks in the region is growing, and all of this ends up being very bullish for volume and for the long-term margin of KOF.

It is also necessary to highlight how healthy the balance sheet is, and this is super important for a company that has debt in countries that have high-interest rates. For the sake of curiosity, the little debt (~$30 million) that KOF had in Argentina (in ARS) in 2024 had a rate of 50% per year. But even with the average rate being ~8.5%, this is not a concern, because the net debt/EBITDA is less than 1x, very comfortable, and is one more reinforcement that what turns into free cash flow ends up being very available to become shareholder yield through buybacks or dividends.

A Word About Valuation and LatAm Uncertainties

Until now, I have only spoken of the positive points of KOF, and as I mentioned at the start of the analysis, Coca-Cola FEMSA is a great company.

But there are other points to consider. Many perhaps see the main problem for KOF as the FX impact, and it really is something very important for the financials. In Q4, for instance, the top line growth was ~6% "adjusted". But the real growth in USD was 2.9%. This is because there was devaluation of some currencies against the USD in this period.

Because of this, it is necessary to compare KOF with companies from Mexico, Brazil, and so on. Its P/E (TTM) of 16x seems low in isolation, but as I know many Brazilian companies, I know it is not exactly cheap.

For instance, the Brazilian index is being traded at a P/E of 12.6x and with an average close to ~11x. The P/E of the Mexican index (EWW) is at 13.7x.

Just these two pieces of information already demystify a bit the assumption that KOF is dirty cheap or something like that.

This ends up being a risk point in my view. I don't think the valuation is bad (which is why the rating is a buy), but if inflation in these countries worsens, there is some macroeconomic pressure, or there is just a worse sentiment in the market, KOF's valuation could get closer to these indexes or even decrease along with these other stocks, i.e., a de-rating in valuation isn't out of the question.

We should also remember that these LatAm ETFs, such as those for Brazilian or Mexican stocks, have many commodity stocks (which naturally trade at a lower P/E). If we were to compare with other staples ETFs, such as KXI (iShares Global Consumer Staples), which is global staples, or PSCC (Invesco S&P SmallCap Consumer Staples), which is small-cap staples, the 'P/Es' are 25x and 20x earnings (both TTM, respectively). In other words, compared with Staples, KOF's valuation already looks more appealing, something like a "Staples" but with a slight discount due to its "Latin America" status.

Speaking of KOF's valuation "alone", the consensus is for an EPS of almost $7 for 2026, i.e., a fwd P/E of 14x. I think that's where KOF's case starts to get a little more interesting. For example, if the scenario continues to improve for emerging markets, such as FX or a more lasting rotation, perhaps there will be some level of re-rating. Of course, I'm not talking here about a premium like Costco (COST); I'm talking about a forward P/E of 18x (in a bullish scenario for LatAm), which already means an upside of about 30%. And this, in my view, makes sense; in good times, more defensive LatAm stocks are traded at slightly higher multiples.

In other words, a re-rating is possible, but we should not see a de-rating as impossible either.

Bottom Line: Good Enough

If we compare it with other peers like Ambev (ABEV), Wal-Mart De Mexico (WMMVY), and Compañía Cervecerías Unidas (CCU) from Chile, we see that KOF's P/E is not that bad. It is in line with other companies in the sector that are very exposed (or only exposed) to Brazil, Chile, and Mexico.

The history of compounding is very good, showing that the company knows how to work in this environment and managed not to lose margin (nor volume) even in a high-inflation environment. In my view, it is a company with more solid prospects than other Brazilian companies like Ambev that do not have a valuation much lower than KOF.

The section above showed a bit of both sides (de-rating and re-rating), but in the end, I think KOF deserves a relative premium because of this. Even with revenue closely tied to LatAm and with risks because of it (FX, inflation) that deserve to be taken into account, we can also follow the inverse reasoning: even in this complex environment, KOF managed to deliver very solid financials and attractive growth with healthy margins, and this needs to be rewarded in some way. Also, the prospects for both top line and bottom line are good, with initiatives like Juntos+ poised to drive some margin expansion. Again, I am not talking about a Costco-level re-rating, but rather taking into account the solidity of this compounding and accepting paying a slight premium compared to other stocks with dependence on Latin America.

Because of this history and characteristic of combining quality with growth, I think the valuation is fair.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Perdoceo Education: A Free Cash Flow Stock The Market Is Missing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Perdoceo Education (PRDO) has delivered strong financial performance in FY 2025, yet the stock is trading at a discount to peers and the sector median. In this analysis, I have gone through the latest earnings results, segment performance, free cash flow generation, management guidance, and peer benchmarking to understand whether this discount is justified. In my view, PRDO is poised to outperform, driven by sustained enrollment growth, expanding operating margins, exceptional free cash flow generation, and continued momentum from the USAHS acquisition. On the valuation side, when we look at the ex-cash forward P/E of 9.0x, the discount deepens further. The cash alone provides a margin of safety of 27% of the current stock price. In my view, the market has not fully recognized this combination, and that is where the opportunity lies for potential upside.

PRDO is a provider of postsecondary education primarily through online programs. It operates through three segments: Colorado Technical University (CTU), the American InterContinental University System (AIUS), and the University of St. Augustine for Health Sciences (USAHS).

Free Cash Flow: Key Catalyst for the Stock

Data in $ million FY 2024 FY 2025
Cash From Operations 161.6 225.2
Growth 39.4%
Capex 4.6 8.6
Free Cash Flow 157.0 216.6
FCF Growth 38.0%
Cash & Investments 591.5 643.5

Data sourced from FY 2025 10-K and Q4 2025 Press Release

In FY 2025, the company generated cash from operations of $225.2 million, an increase of 39.4% from the prior year. The nature of online education is asset-light compared to a traditional university. They do not need to build large physical campuses and associated infrastructure. The primary investment is in technology platforms, content, and talented people. To meet such requirements, the company needs a capital expenditure of only $8.6 million. That means the company is spending just 1% of revenue to generate FCF of $216.6 million in FY 2025 with an FCF margin of 25.1% as a percentage of revenue.

So far I have just discussed how much this company is generating FCF from its revenue, which already seems attractive. But here is the valuation insight for investors. The cash and short-term investments stood at $643.5 million in FY 2025. Based on the share price of $37.74, the market capitalization is ~$2.3 billion and the enterprise value (EV) excluding cash is $1.7 billion. An implied EV/Adjusted Operating Income of approximately 7.2x is calculated based on adjusted operating income of $237.6 million. For an online business growing adjusted operating income by 25.8%, is this not an extraordinary set of indicators? Furthermore, FCF of $216.6 million on top of this makes the case even stronger.

Based on 62.4 million shares outstanding, the cash per share is $10.3. The stock price gives an ex-cash price of $27.4. That means the cash alone provides a margin of safety of 27% of the stock price. The management has guided an adjusted EPS midpoint of $3.04 for FY2026, which implies an ex-cash forward P/E of 9.0x.

The stock already trades at a forward P/E of 12.30x, which is at a discount to the sector median of 14.64x. When you apply the ex-cash adjustment, the discount deepens further to 9.0x.

Based on this analysis, the cash alone provides a significant margin of safety to investors. When you exclude the cash and look at the implied ex-cash forward P/E of 9.0x, what investors are actually paying for the operating business. In my view, this is a hidden opportunity that deserves market attention.

Diversified Business Model: Drives Revenue and Operating Income Growth

To better understand the revenue growth and margins, it's important to do an analysis of segments.

CTU is the largest and most profitable segment. It offers courses in business and technology to working adult students. In FY 2025, revenue increased 4.1% to $461.6 million, driven by student enrollment growth of 6.6% to 29,950 students. For investors, the enrollment growth is the most important number to track. As enrolled students progress through their multi-semester programs, the revenue contribution flows in subsequent quarters. Operating income increased 3.4% to $180.5 million with an operating margin of 39.1%. In a nutshell, CTU is a cash cow with high revenue, consistent operating income, and minimal capex requirements.

AIUS shows a different trend, and it is important for investors to understand why. Revenue for FY 2025 was $226.2 million, down marginally from $227.1 million in FY 2024. However, enrollment has a higher growth rate across all three segments at 11.2%, to 10,560 students. The natural question is how can enrollment grow 11.2% while revenue is flat? This is the enrollment lag effect. Students who enroll in a semester have their full revenue contribution flow in subsequent semesters as they progress through their programs. In other words, AIUS's strong enrollment growth in FY 2025 is not yet recognized in the FY 2025 revenue. It will show up in FY 2026 and beyond. In a nutshell, the 11.2% enrollment growth is a leading indicator for investors to gauge future revenue. Furthermore, operating income grew 9.8% to $36.0 million. The growth in operating income is faster than revenue growth, which shows improving operational efficiency within the segment. This is the most important indicator of this segment, as it shows operational efficiency and cash generation capability.

USAHS is the most important segment story for this fiscal year. It was acquired in December 2024. In FY 2025, it contributed $157.6 million in revenue and generated $3.2 million in operating income. This represents a swing of $5.8 million from a $2.6 million operating loss to profitability in a year. The programs offered are in graduate health sciences fields with specific professional licensing requirements that create natural demand and strong pricing power. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, employment in physical therapy is projected to grow 11% through 2034. This demand scenario will support enrollment growth for this segment, which in turn drives the segment's performance in the years ahead.

Based on the segment analysis, we can conclude that PRDO has three distinct but complementary pillars of growth and FCF generation. The investors should look for student enrollment and operating margin growth in the next earnings to evaluate the performance.

FY2026 Guidance: Management is Signaling Continued Acceleration

In the above analysis, we have seen how the three segments are growing along with an improving margin profile. The management guidance for FY 2026 further substantiates our investment thesis and gives investors confidence that this momentum will continue for next year.

For Q1 2026, management guided $0.83 to $0.85 in adjusted EPS compared to $0.70 actual in Q1 2025, an increase of 18 to 21% growth in the first quarter itself. On a yearly basis, adjusted EPS of $2.97 to $3.12 represents a 14 to 20% growth on FY 2025's $2.61. Investors should understand that this is a business with double-digit earnings growth, which had already achieved 25.8% adjusted operating income growth in the previous year. It shows that the operational momentum is strong and that management has clear visibility into the revenue growth and cost structure.

Online Education Market: Structural Tailwinds Support Long-Term Demand

The online education market is growing and has structural tailwinds that directly support the investment thesis for PRDO.

According to Grand View Research, the global e-learning services market is projected to reach $842.64 billion by 2030 from $299.67 billion in 2024, growing at a CAGR of 19.0%. This growth is driven by the increasing requirement for upskilling, demand for flexible learning, and career advancement for working people.

As we can see from the chart above, the age group of over 30 consists of 67% of the company's revenue. This signifies that the product offering is directly aligned with the broader trend of online education and positions the company to grow further over the next 5 years.

To capture market share in this growing market, PRDO is well-equipped with its Intellipath learning platform. This platform is a proprietary adaptive learning technology that personalizes course content and creates a customized workflow based on each enrolled student's performance data.

As CEO Todd Nelson said in the Q4 2025 earnings report:

I am proud of the educational achievements at our academic institutions that have enabled us to deliver strong 2025 results, which were supported by sustained levels of prospective student interest in our programs and high levels of student retention across all our academic institutions.

Management highlighted student retention as a key driver of enrollment growth in FY 2025. The Intellipath platform creates a personalized student experience that supports retention and reduces dropout rates. A student who stays enrolled generates tuition revenue across multiple semesters at near-zero incremental acquisition cost. This is the direct link between the technology platform and free cash flow quality, in which better retention means more recurring revenue with no additional marketing spend.

In a nutshell, this combination of market tailwinds and a technology platform is what makes the long-term investment case for PRDO genuinely strong.

Relative Valuation: Trading at a Discount Despite Superior Growth

Peers Forward P/E

Forward EV/EBITDA

Revenue Growth
Perdoceo Education (PRDO) 12.30x 7.18x 24.20%
Grand Canyon Education (LOPE) 16.98x 11.12x 7.07%
Strategic Education (STRA) 11.95x 6.17x 3.96%
Peer Average 13.74x 8.15x
Sector Median 14.64x 9.43x

Data sourced from Seeking Alpha

Based on the above analysis, PRDO is trading at a forward P/E of 12.30x with a discount to both the peer average of 13.74x and the sector median of 14.64x. On a forward EV/EBITDA basis, it trades at 7.18x compared to the peer average of 8.15x and the sector median of 9.43x. Both comparable valuation metrics confirm the stock is trading at a discount relative to its peers and the broader sector. Further, both peers have less revenue growth in FY 2025. In a nutshell, investors are paying a lower multiple for a business growing significantly faster than its peers.

Investment Risk

Enrollment Growth Deceleration - Enrollment is a key metric for tracking the company’s performance. The FCF thesis depends on sustained enrollment growth and high student retention. If enrollment growth slows significantly due to competition, regulatory changes, or weaker demand, FCF growth could be adversely affected.

USAHS Integration Risk - Although USAHS has become profitable, the full integration process is still underway. Any unexpected integration costs or issues with student outcomes could negatively affect FCF due to underperformance in this segment.

Technology and Program Relevance Risk - Online education platforms require continuous investment in technology, curriculum updates, and student engagement tools. If the company fails to adapt to student preferences and technological trends, it could lose market share. This will lead to lower revenue and potential pressure on the FCF thesis.

Final Thoughts

PRDO is trading at a forward P/E of 12.30x with a discount to both the peer average and the sector median. Furthermore, when you apply the ex-cash forward P/E of 9.0x, it becomes even more compelling with a margin of safety of 27%. In my view, this combination of superior revenue growth, double-digit earnings growth guidance, FCF generation, and a discounted valuation multiple makes PRDO one of the most attractive risk-reward opportunities in the education sector today.

data.title: BK Technologies: The Shift To Software Could Change Everything
data.description:
Investment thesis

Note: This is an update to my previous article. In this coverage, I'm upgrading from Hold to Buy because the growth prospects have improved dramatically and the margin expansion story isn't over yet.

BK Technologies (BKTI) is transitioning from a traditional radio manufacturer to a higher quality company with recurring revenue from its software. Recent results show accelerating growth and a continuation of its margin expansion story. The goal for 2030 is to increase revenue by approximately 15% annually, along with gross margins of 60% compared to the current 50%.

If you have a long-term vision and trust the management, which has a track record of exceeding expectations, I consider it a buy at the current price.

Margin expansion continues

This quarter was very interesting for BKTI because they reported the close of fiscal year 2025 and also held their Investor Day with some very valuable information. Let's start with their Q4 2025 report, which they shared in March. They reported sales of $21.51 million, representing a 10% beat of analysts expectations and 20% growth! This surprised me because it marks the second consecutive quarter of growth exceeding 20%, something I hadn't considered in my previous coverage when I didn't see the company as a growth story. More important, this quarter's 20% growth comes on top of a 10% growth in Q4 2024, which was already the highest growth of 2023 and 2024.

And in case you're wondering, I couldn't find any one-off driving the EPS beat, so I guess that came mostly from operating leverage rather than accounting noise.

Q4 2025 Estimate Actual Beat/miss
Revenue $19.50M $21.51M 10.3%
EPS $0.53 $1.06 100.0%

Author's compilation

For fiscal year 2026, the initial guidance is at least $90 million in sales and gross margins of 50%. Don't be surprised if those are easily surpassed, since in 2025 the company had given guidance of single-digit growth and gross margins of at least 42%, while it ended up growing 12.5% ​​with margins of 48.8% and the most recent quarter even reached a 50% gross margin. A constant beat and raise from the company. I really don't like management that overpromises because even if they deliver, the risk of them failing in the next quarter of the year is very high, but this is not the case for BKTI.

The 2030 vision

As I mentioned earlier, the company also held its Investor Day, where it shared its vision for the next four years (I know, 2030 sounds far off, but it's not so far anymore). The goal is ambitious, to say the least:

  1. Double sales to $170 million, so 15% annualized growth.
  2. Gross margins of 60% vs. 50% in the last quarter.
  3. EBITDA margins of 35% vs. 22% in the most recent quarter.

Given the company's track record of meeting its goals, however ambitious they may seem, I believe we can consider that the goal is not so unrealistic. Remember that in my first coverage, the gross margin was only 38% (now 50%), and the company's recent results with high growth and further margin expansion show good execution. Logically, it's worth asking where this growth will come from. And here's where it gets interesting, because this is not a "let's just sell more radios" story.

One of the levers for growth is expanding their market to what they call "Tier 3" clients, which are smaller state and local agencies that are more price sensitive because they are less tied to traditional suppliers (like Motorola) and prioritize the cost/benefit over brand, which is a perfect fit for a small player without a well-known brand like BK.

In numbers, the company estimates there are around 29,000 Public Safety Agencies and more than 2.5 million potential users, so simply capturing a portion of that market share would represent considerable growth for such a small company. In fact, the company estimates its total addressable market in the USA/Canada to be valued at $2.4 billion, and it aims to capture 4.5% of that market by 2030, compared to its current estimate of around 3.6%.

But the growth lever that most intrigues me is their attempt to shift the business toward software with products like InteropONE, LocateONE, and their "BK ONE" ecosystem, designed to work on top of their hardware and provide more functionality to the equipment. If this is successful, I believe it could help explain their margin goal, since hardware alone won't likely allow them to reach their expected gross margins, but software could easily do so while also adding recurring revenue and improving the company's quality overall.

Valuation

Looking ahead, I estimate that sales could grow by 12% annually over the next few years and margins could increase from 16% to 20%, considering that the most recent quarter already posted net margins of 20%. While I know the goal is 15% annual revenue growth, I prefer to have a safety margin, hence my modest 12% estimate.

With these assumptions, EPS for 2030 would be around $7.70, and if the market is willing to pay a multiple of 20 times for this reinvented business that has returned to growth with more recurring revenues and high margins, then the share price could be around $150-155 compared to the current $80-85 (even taking into account the 10% increase after Investor's Day). That's an investment return of over 12% annualized.

Now, let's consider some scenarios. If the company were to grow its sales by 15% annually and we maintain our margin and multiple assumptions, the return on investment would be around 15-16%. A more compelling case.

However, if growth falls short, margins don't expand sufficiently, and therefore the market is only willing to pay a market average multiple of 15 times, it wouldn't be surprising to see a flat stock price for five years. I'm not saying this is my base case scenario, but it's good to keep in mind what could go wrong.

Risks

The most obvious risk at this point is that the company's success depends on the smooth execution of the new software. Much of its long-term margin expansion goal seems to depend on BK successfully integrating its software and making it appealing to its customers. The roadmap is clear, but developing it is one thing, and getting customers to adopt and, even more important, be willing to pay for it is another. If this transition takes longer than expected or simply fails, margin expansion may not materialize and current expectations won't be met.

I also think there's some competitive pressure, since the market is dominated by companies like Motorola with approximately 75% market share, and BK's positioning as a budget alternative is being successful in some niches, but it also makes me wonder: is this advantage sustainable? Competing aggressively on price is rarely the best option, and it is usually best to offer added value. Maybe this is why BK is looking to add features that differentiate it from simply offering radios, but again, this means the thesis revolves around creating software and additional features that are interesting enough for its customers.

The bottom line

The company continues to execute, and the story is increasingly transitioning toward accelerating growth, recurring revenue, and margin expansion through software. While the valuation may not be particularly cheap and the multiple seems high, if you have a long-term vision, I wouldn't recommend missing out on a company with a long track record of under-promising and exceeding expectations.

The roadmap is clear, and there are reasons to believe it can be delivered. But it's clear that the biggest risk will be successfully executing the plan.

data.title: Kratos Defense: The Upside Is Real, The Risk Is Too
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The primary reason for my interest in reviewing Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc.'s (KTOS) "dossier" now is because of a Buy recommendation issued by Jefferies. A price target of $85 was also established for the Company’s shares. Since the shares are currently trading around $72, this target price represents a possible upside of nearly 18%. That caused me to review the Company’s fundamentals further.

I previously reviewed Kratos toward the end of 2025 and ultimately concluded with a Hold rating. At that time, I felt that the Company's latest quarterly report showed some strong performance; however, I believed that its valuation was already quite high, and I questioned whether the Company generated sufficient cash flow. Since that time, the Company's stock price has declined from approximately $76.23 to $71.96, a decline of almost 5.6%.

At present, I believe that there is little difference in the overall narrative surrounding Kratos today versus what I viewed a few months ago. Today, I perceive Kratos as a company whose ability to grow going forward has been heightened relative to where we were a few months ago. Nevertheless, I still view the Company as being subject to significant execution risks. To clarify, the potential exists for growth, although the environment does not lend itself to building a large position based upon reasonable expectations.

From a portfolio perspective, my analysis of Kratos will continue to reflect the same thinking as before. Therefore, if Kratos makes sense as a small speculative investment position, I may choose to invest a small amount of money into it (as a controlled speculation). However, I am not prepared to build a larger position in the company, as it is characterized by excessive volatility.

The Part That's Holding Me Back

While revenue growth hasn’t been a problem (Kratos’ financials have continued to grow), the reason why Kratos has lagged up until now lies somewhere else. Mainly, the question isn’t how fast Kratos grows – it’s how fast it generates that revenue and how long does it takes before that revenue translates into actual cash.

In particular, we know from the following Bloomberg chart that free cash flow performance is still poor. Capex levels are high, and although forecasts suggest that things will get better over the next few years, free cash flow remains negative. This indicates that, certainly, Kratos is generating revenue — however, it is generating that revenue at a time when it’s still using many of those resources internally.

One statistic gives us far more insight into the overall quality of the growth than most others. The statistic is called free cash flow margin. In essence, it is the ratio of free cash flow to revenue. Formula? Simple: Take free cash flow. Divide it by revenue. Multiply by 100. It tells us both where revenue is going and what percent of revenue is ultimately turning into cash. When I look at Kratos’ free cash flow margin over the past twelve months (-10.2%), it suggests that the company burned roughly 10 cents of free cash flow for every dollar of revenue generated during the same period. To determine whether this was simply temporary or something more structural, I looked at Kratos’ free cash flow margin over the past five years. When I averaged those values, I found an average of -3.6%. I realized that not only was the current value less than the historic average — but also the forecast for future years remained in the red.

Thus, without further ado, it seems safe to conclude that Kratos is currently converting a worse percentage of revenue into cash than it has historically. This reality would make the stock vulnerable. Until the free cash flow margin shows credible normalization, an operational slowdown, a delay in programs, or a disappointment in margins is enough to put pressure on a valuation that remains delicate.

The point, in my opinion, is that this expansion phase has a very real cost in terms of cash. While Kratos has indeed invested substantially to support this growth — and that investment is evident in the company’s free cash flow profile — if you were to add together roughly $130 million in cap-ex and another $25-30 million in working capital spend, you’d find yourself with a net cash burn equal to approximately $80 million. As J.P. Morgan wrote in a February 2025 note, several projects are driving elevated investment, including MACH-TB 2.0 and facilities for microwave products, space, and hypersonics. Most of these investments were expected to be completed by 2026, although maintenance capex was still expected to remain above $35 million thereafter.

Therefore, while Kratos' growth exists, the company is still consuming substantial amounts of cash to generate that growth. Moreover, given the potential for capex to be higher in 2026, it seems unlikely that getting to clean(er) cash flows would be anything like a straight line.

Contracts, Margins, and Concentration

The risks of the margin area are present, and many of these issues will be more challenging to address. As mentioned previously, Kratos has been in many long-term contracts which failed to account for the inflationary cost environment experienced during the last few years, and many of those costs were associated with drone target costs. Therefore, regardless of the revenue growth, the margins will take much longer to begin expanding as they did during previous times. J.P. Morgan also expects potential margin expansion of 100-150 bps annually going forward; however, this remains contingent on execution under newer contracts. Thus, it is not a given or automatic process.

I would like to point out a second risk which I feel the markets often overlook when evaluating companies such as Kratos Defense & Security Solutions: concentration. Although the largest customer is the U.S. Government (accounting for 68% of its total revenue), and while this represents a large portion of the company’s revenue stream — it does represent an extremely high level of exposure to how the government conducts business, including the timing and approval processes of appropriations, continuing resolutions, and even simply awarding of contract delays.

Finally, there is a small risk that should be monitored concerning the timing of the expiration of a government contract. The Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) Phase III contract expires on December 10, 2026, and based on historical trends over the last nearly six years, it averages approximately 4.3% of revenue annually, with each order averaging approximately $58.44 million. While this will not significantly alter the course of Kratos’ history, it is a reminder that a substantial amount of the company revenue is dependent on renewal and/or award decisions from either competitive processes and/or government agencies, which are ultimately not fully controlled by the Company.

Stretched Valuation

My Hold rating is based on a simple point: the market has already priced in a lot of upside.

Using the Bloomberg multiple model, Kratos is priced significantly above its peers. For instance, Kratos' forward EV/EBITDA is approximately 72.1X compared to about 25.5X for comparable peer group companies. A significant differential exists between these two multiple models. It says one thing; the market has already incorporated the expectation of very high levels of growth for the Company going into the future.

To gain perspective on how expensive the current valuation price of Kratos is relative to others, compare Kratos' valuation to some of its peers: L3Harris at 21.8X (LHX), Northrop Grumman (NOC) at 17.6X, Curtiss Wright (CW) at 32.9X, and other names.

The P/E ratio follows suit. According to Bloomberg, Kratos' forward P/E ratio is approximately 94.7X for fiscal year 2026 and approximately 69.1X for fiscal year 2027. Both are extremely high P/E ratios, indicating historically low earnings and the fact that most of the investment thesis is based upon what the company is expected to accomplish in the future, not what it currently produces today.

Why $85 Isn't Enough to Call a Buy

At this stage, we can take seriously the question: If I believe this stock will go to $85, why don’t I recommend a Buy? An $85 target price tells us that there’s plausibility to higher prices. However, it does not suggest that the expected return/risk ratio is sufficient to justify recommending a Buy.

A simple way to look at it would be: Bloomberg has estimated Kratos’ 2028 EBITDA as being close to $271 million. If the market were to continue to see the stock based on “growth-defense” multiples (in the range of about 57-60x EV/EBITDA), then it seems entirely possible that the stock could move up to the $85 level without a further multiple expansion. To put it simply, no miracles are required.

All that is necessary is for the Company to perform well and for time to allow those numbers to come into play. The issue is that this outcome may not occur without some risk. Getting there requires operational continuity, growth drivers need to become more concrete and more closely tied to real contracts, and the market also needs to maintain its faith in history. While this isn’t necessarily an impossible route, it’s certainly not a straightforward one.

Conclusion

If investors continue to see Kratos as a company which can grow much faster than the norm in the defense industry, and those specific areas like Hypersonics, Drones, Advanced Electronics & Space begin to show increasing amounts of tangible and verifiable growth through contract awards, the stock could potentially hit the $85 price point. Furthermore, the potential for the stock reaching $85 doesn’t have to be from another multiple expansion but simply due to the fact that as time moves forward, investors will increasingly pay attention to the numbers associated with 2027 and 2028.

However, I’m still hesitant to call it a Buy. The costs needed to sustain the current rate of growth are extremely high ($CapEx + Working Capital Absorption). The cash flow generated has been and is fragile. The valuation is also extremely stretched. To say it differently, I believe that while there may be some upside, the Margin For Error remains low.

As such, in my opinion, Kratos should be viewed with caution. If one does decide to participate, they should do so in a limited manner. The goal would be to capture some or all of the potential upside, while keeping in mind that volatility is not a blip in this story; it’s part of the package.

data.title: Tesla Q1 2026 Deliveries Strengthen The Bearish Outlook
data.description:
Background

On April 2, 2026, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) released its Q1 production and delivery report, which showed several challenges for the company. Vehicle deliveries were below expectations, while production exceeded deliveries, pushing inventory to a record high. At the same time, energy storage deployments missed estimates by a wide margin. If this trend continues, achieving the FY 2026 consensus will be difficult and will support the bear thesis.

Q1 2026 Delivery Report Analysis

1. Deliveries:

Deliveries Q12025 Q42025 Q12026 Street Consensus
Total Vehicles 336,681 418,227 358,023 365,645
YoY Change 6.3%
QoQ Change -14.4%
vs. Consensus Missed -7,622

Data compiled by the author using the Tesla delivery report and analyst consensus compiled by the company.

For Q1 2026, the company delivered 358,023 vehicles, missing the street consensus of 365,645 vehicles by 7,622 units. The company-compiled consensus was based on estimates from 23 sell-side analysts, including Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and JP Morgan.

The 6.3% year-over-year increase may seem good at first. But that's not the case here. Q1 2025 included several weeks of production disruption due to the changeover of Model Y lines across all four global factories. Hence, the comparison with Q1 2025 is not like-for-like. Therefore, comparing against a weak quarter and still missing consensus is not a positive signal. On a quarterly basis, deliveries declined by 14.4% from Q4 2025.

The missed expectations seem to be due to company-specific issues in key markets rather than a global slowdown in EV demand. Tesla generates its highest revenue from the U.S., followed by China and the rest of the world. Any issues in the U.S. or China can therefore have a significant impact on overall demand for Tesla. The U.S. $7,500 EV tax credit expired in September 2025, reducing demand. According to Cox Automotive, new EV sales in the U.S. market declined by 28% in Q1 2026 compared to Q1 2025. In China, stiff competition from domestic manufacturers is creating further challenges for the company.

2. Production:

Production Q12025 Q42025 Q12026
Total Vehicles Produced 362,615 434,358 408,386
YoY Change 12.6%
QoQ Change -6.0%
Inventory Build 25,934 16,131 50,363

Data compiled by the author using the Tesla delivery report.

In terms of production, it produced 408,386 vehicles in Q1 2026 compared to a delivery of 358,023. This gap is the key point for investors, as it added 50,363 vehicles to inventory in the quarter. A mismatch between supply and demand may not be temporary and could be structural. Investors should watch for this in the next report. On a quarterly basis, production declined 6.0% from Q4 2025, when 434,358 vehicles were made. On a yearly basis, production increased 12.6% compared with a 6.3% growth in deliveries. Further, the gap between production growth and delivery growth is not a positive indicator, as the excess goes into inventory. In a nutshell, comparing deliveries and production highlights a structural crack in Tesla’s bull case due to inventory buildup.

3. Energy Storage:

Energy Storage Q12025 Q42025 Q12026 Street Consensus
Deployments (GWH) 10.4 14.2 8.8 14.4
YoY Change -15.4%
QoQ Change -38.0%
vs. Consensus Missed -38.9%

Data compiled by the author using the Tesla delivery report and analyst consensus compiled by the company.

Energy storage deployed was 8.8 GWh in Q1 2026 as compared to a consensus expectation of 14.4 GWh, missing estimates by 38.9%. This represents a 38.0% decline from Q4 2025 and a 15.4% drop from Q1 2025. In my view, this is a very weak performance.

The company reports revenue in three segments: Automotive, Energy Generation and Storage, and Services. Based on the Q1 delivery report, two of these segments are underperforming. This supports the bear investment thesis.

Recovery Looks Challenging In FY 2026

To understand the challenges Tesla faces, investors should look at key metrics on an annual basis. The trend suggests the company may have difficulty achieving its FY 2026 targets in the remaining quarters.

FY2023 FY2024 FY2025 FY2026 Consensus
Total Deliveries 1,808,581 1,789,226 1,636,129 1,689,691
YoY Change -1.1% -8.6% 3.3%
Total Production 1,654,667
Inventory Build 18,538
Deployments (GWH) 46.7 65.2

Data compiled by the author using the Tesla annual delivery reports and analyst consensus compiled by the company.

The company had its highest deliveries in FY 2023 at 1.81 million vehicles. Since then, performance has fallen for two years, with 1.79 million in FY 2024 and 1.64 million in FY 2025. The consensus for FY 2026 is 1.69 million vehicles, showing a 3.3% upside. The Q1 2026 delivery data make this target harder to reach. The company now needs about 444,000 deliveries per quarter to meet the full-year goal. This looks very difficult given recent performance.

Inventory Build Is Getting Worse

Inventory Build Q12024 Q12025 Q22025 Q42025 Q12026
Deliveries 386,810 336,681 384,122 418,227 358,023
Production 433,371 362,615 410,244 434,358 408,386
Inventory Build -46,561 -25,934 -26,122 -16,131 -50,363

Data compiled by the author using the Tesla delivery reports.

In Q1 2026, the company had a production-to-delivery gap, which is not new for Tesla. This repeated pattern is now widening, suggesting a structural issue driven by softening demand. In Q1 2024, production exceeded deliveries by 46,561 vehicles. The same trend continued in Q1 2025 and Q2 2025, with inventory building by around 25,000 vehicles each quarter.

Q4 2025 was slightly encouraging as the inventory gap fell to 16,131 vehicles. But Q1 2026 surpassed the previous high from Q1 2024, reporting the largest quarterly inventory build at 50,363 vehicles. It appears that the minimal-inventory model of producing to order is now broken for Tesla. This points to a demand problem with a sustained inventory build across multiple quarters. Any further deterioration from here could be a key bear catalyst for the company.

Energy Storage Is Now Under Pressure

Energy Storage (GWH) FY2024 FY2025 FY2026
Deployments 31.4 46.7 65.2
YoY Change 48.7% 39.6%

Data compiled by the author using the Tesla delivery reports and analyst consensus compiled by the company.

Energy storage numbers in the Q1 2026 report were a setback for investors who were looking for positive trends amid declining vehicle deliveries. In FY 2025, energy storage deployments reached 46.7 GWh, up from 31.4 GWh in FY 2024, with an increase of 48.7%. This strong growth had given investors hope that the energy business could deliver growth even as vehicle deliveries struggled.

However, Q1 2026 has put that momentum under pressure. Energy storage is 8.8 GWh below the consensus of 14.4 GWh. This creates a significant gap for the FY 2026 consensus of 65.2 GWh. To meet that target, the company now needs to deploy 56.4 GWh across the remaining three quarters, or about 18.8 GWh per quarter.

Hence, Tesla will need to exceed the Q4 2025 record of 14.2 GWh in each of the remaining quarters of FY 2026. This is a challenging task and a key metric to watch in the next quarter.

Valuation Is Too High For The Fundamentals

The stock trades at 171x forward earnings based on FY 2026 Street consensus EPS of $2.08. In my view, this valuation is hard to justify for a company having two consecutive years of delivery decline with a record inventory build and a significant miss in energy storage for Q1 2026. The current valuation is based on the bull case that Tesla can transform from an automotive-focused company into a technology platform generating revenue from autonomous driving, robotics, and AI services. With the core business already under pressure, these assumptions look increasingly risky, supporting the bear thesis around Tesla’s story.

Final Thoughts

Tesla has seen deliveries decline for two years in a row, and inventory is at a record high. On top of that, energy storage missed consensus by a wide margin in Q1. All of this makes the FY 2026 target difficult to achieve without a significant increase in demand. Investors should track Q2 2026 deliveries to see how things are trending and whether the bear case is gaining strength. Further, the Q1 2026 earnings call on April 22 will show if management has a plan to recover or if the bear case gets stronger.

data.title: Enviri: A 'Strong Buy' As Shares Remain Deeply Discounted
data.description:
Rarely have I been as bullish about a company as I am right now about Enviri Corporation (NVRI). The last article that I wrote about the company was published in November of last year. At that time, the stock had benefited tremendously from news that management was selling off the company’s Clean Earth segment in a deal valued at $3.04 billion. This was monumental considering that the enterprise value of the firm at that time was just $2.85 billion. To be honest with you, I'm surprised the stock hasn't moved up considerably higher from where it is now. After all, shares look incredibly cheap, and implied upside potential is massive. That deal has yet to close, but it does look imminent. And when it does, the total value coming to shareholders should be quite large. This is why I have been actively investing in the stock. It's not my largest position by any means. But it is large enough that, if I do turn out to be accurate, it would have an outsized positive impact on my portfolio.

Taking a Fresh Look at Enviri Corporation

To be honest with you, I don't think it would be terribly useful to rehash all the details regarding the aforementioned transaction whereby Enviri Corporation will be selling off its Clean Earth business in a transaction worth $3.04 billion. I already covered that in great depth in my previous article. However, a bit of a refresher is probably needed. According to the terms of the agreement, Enviri Corporation will be receiving $2.975 billion in cash, plus the buyer will be assuming $65 million of debt and debt-like obligations. From those proceeds, management will allocate $1.35 billion to pay down net debt. They will also incur between $110 million and $150 million of transaction-related costs and adjustments. You also have to factor in between $60 million and $200 million for certain obligations that it will have to cover for its rail business. But after all of this is said and done, the company should make it possible to pay cash to shareholders of between $1.275 billion and $1.455 billion. This translates to between $14.50 per share and $16.50 per share in distributions.

Considering that the business is currently trading at $19.80 per share, this means that what is left over from the transaction is currently being valued at the spread that exists here. At the midpoint, this would mean about $4.30 per share. And with 88 million shares outstanding, that implies an equity value for the company of $378.4 million. At the time that the transaction closes, the business should have net debt, based on my own calculations, of about $92.3 million. In my previous article, I detailed how revenue for the remaining company, which will involve Harsco Environmental and Harsco Rail, will be around $1.27 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA should come in somewhere around $140.5 million. This is based on management's guidance for 2026 for the company that excludes Clean Earth. It also happens to be above the $136 million that the company previously calculated using estimates for the 2025 fiscal year.

When you add all of this together, you have a firm with a pro forma enterprise value of $470.7 million. That means an EV-to-EBITDA multiple of 3.35. For a firm with a net leverage ratio at closing of just 0.66, that is incredibly low. Now it is worth pointing out that management said that they intend to let leverage increase. The goal is to grow this to 2, which would mean the eventual assumption of an additional $188.7 million of net debt. But because we don't know precisely what the company will be using that capital for, I am going to assume that this does not transpire since allocating that capital could theoretically change the overall financial picture of the business quite a bit from a revenue, profit, and cash flow perspective.

While analyzing the company, I decided to look at six other similar firms. In the chart above, you can see how these companies are valued on both an EV-to-EBITDA and EV-to-revenue basis. If we take the average of these multiples and price the company accordingly, we would be looking at additional value above the share price of the company of between $9.14 per share and $13.23 per share. This translates to an upside of between 46.1% and 66.8%, based on where the stock is trading at this moment. However, the ultimate return for investors could end up being quite a bit higher. That's because this analysis factors in a return on investment from buying today.

The market is currently valuing the stub, or what will remain of the business, at $4.30 per share. The business, after the transaction, should be worth between $13.44 and $17.53. If the stock is in fact trading at the current implied price after the transaction closes, just buying the firm then for around $4.30 would offer significantly greater potential upside, ranging between 212.4% and 307.6%. This is actually lower than what I previously forecasted. But in the time that the stock has traded since then, shares have risen, essentially eating away at the easiest of the gains. Not buying now creates the risk of missing out on further upside since, in theory, you would expect shares to converge closer to their fair value range.

I would argue, however, that upside potential could be even greater than this. I say this because the current net leverage ratio picture for the company is very attractive. As I mentioned already, we are looking at a net leverage ratio of 0.66. That is lower than any of the six firms that I compared it to. If anything, I would argue that the business deserves a trading multiple that is greater than what some of these other firms are going for. If we instead say that it should trade at the higher end of the range, then using the EV-to-EBITDA approach to valuing the company would mean a price per share of $23.69. And using the EV-to-revenue approach would mean a price per share of $35.26. Based on the pro forma picture of the business, this would translate to an upside of between 450.8% and 720.1%, or an upside from where the stock is at the moment of between 97.9% and 156.4% by buying today.

This is even though, as the first image below illustrates, Harsco Rail has recently seen a bit of weakness because of lower equipment and aftermarket parts volumes, and it can be justified from continued strength associated with Harsco Environmental. As the second image below illustrates, it has actually seen an improvement in profitability in the most recent quarter, with the firm benefiting from a 7% rise in revenue as services demand increased. Seeing as how this side of the business makes up the overwhelming chunk of the value of the enterprise, that is what deserves the most attention right now.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available, it is my assertion that the market is underestimating Enviri Corporation. Shares of the company are still trading drastically lower than what they should be trading for, even if the implied value for the stub has been eaten away at because of share price appreciation since my previous article. I would make the case, given the quality of its balance sheet and the continued growth associated with Harsco Environmental, that the stock justifies a premium to most other similar firms. But even if the upside matches more similar to what we would see on an average basis, the picture is attractive enough to justify a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: First Industrial Realty Q1 Preview: Continued Momentum Expected, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) is one industrial REIT that has flown somewhat under the radar. The stock is up nearly 30% over the past year, including a 2.75% increase YTD.

While the YTD increase doesn’t sound like much, it’s impressive relative to broader market performance, which is currently in the red due to the ongoing volatility surrounding the war with Iran.

FR will be releasing its Q1 results on April 15, and ahead of the release, I believe shares are fairly valued.

While the REIT certainly has some positive catalysts ahead, I see these as mostly priced into the stock at current trading prices.

FR Stock Key Metrics

While FR is a fairly lightly covered stock, those who have provided coverage are mostly bullish on its prospects. Over the past three years, for example, there has been a near-consensus "bullish" outlook from the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) analyst community.

This sentiment has been echoed by Wall Street. Among this community, about two-thirds of analysts covering the stock over the last 90 days have rated shares as either a "Buy" or a "Strong Buy."

Furthermore, this has been accompanied by an average price target that indicates about 10% upside potential from current trading levels.

While I view that target as attainable, it doesn’t really inspire much excitement from me, especially when there could be more lucrative opportunities elsewhere as a result of the current market pullback.

My sentiment is similar to SA’s quants, which grade FR positively on all metrics except for valuation. I view this as justified, especially in light of FR’s current valuation as measured through price to funds from operations (“FFO”).

At present, FR commands a forward multiple of about 19x. This is in line with the multiple commanded by its larger peer in EastGroup Properties (EGP) but well above the valuations of both Rexford Industrial (REXR) and Stag Industrial (STAG), each of which trades around the 14x mark.

What Is The Outlook For FR Stock?

I believe the outlook for FR is positive, and this was reflected in FR’s most recently completed results for Q4.

Based off the results from Q4, as well as from FR’s recent investor presentation, leasing trends nationally remain active, construction starts are still well below prior peaks, and pre-leasing on new supply is hovering around 40%. These are all positive fundamentals, in my view.

And inside FR’s own portfolio, touring activity is improving, development leasing is picking up, and cash rental rate spreads remain hot, running in the 30%–40% range on new and renewal deals.

In addition, annual escalators in the mid-3% range are now embedded across much of the book, providing visible internal growth into 2026 and beyond.

FR’s balance sheet is also strategically positioned, in my view. Recent term loan refinancings, for example, extended maturities and reduced pricing friction, leaving liquidity in good shape to fund developments at projected 7% yields.

In terms of guidance for 2026, the team is calling for mid-single-digit same-store NOI growth and another year of double-digit FFO/share expansion, supported by lease-ups in the second half of the year.

One item that I’ve noted as a potential watch is governance. Activist investor Jonathan Litt of Land & Buildings recently announced plans to vote against two long-tenured directors, arguing FR trades at a “governance discount” relative to peers like EGP and PLD based on implied cap rates.

While operations are clearly performing, I believe this added pressure introduces a new narrative that could influence investor perception and, potentially, valuation over time.

What To Watch When FR Reports Q1 Results?

When FR reports Q1, I’ll be focused primarily on the underlying leasing signals and what these tell us about where industrial demand is headed.

Sector-wide, vacancy has drifted higher as new supply has been delivered over the past two years, but construction starts remain well below peak levels, and pre-leasing is still healthy.

This should begin to favor landlords again as 2026 progresses, and FR’s commentary around touring activity, lease negotiations, and development interest will be key tells.

I’ll also be watching occupancy closely against the company’s 94%–95% guidance range. Any early progress on lease-ups tied to developments or the large Central Pennsylvania space slated for the back half of the year would be a big positive, in my view.

Equally important will be cash rental rate spreads. FR has been posting impressive 30%+ increases on new and renewal leasing, and confirmation that spreads remain elevated would likely support the internal growth story.

Finally, I want to hear how management frames same-store NOI expectations in the context of moderating supply, embedded 3%+ escalators, and a still-active 3PL and retail tenant base. If those pillars remain intact, the broader sector outlook would likely continue to work in FR’s favor.

Is FR Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

Ahead of FR’s Q1, I view shares as a "Hold."

Leasing spreads remain strong, occupancy is healthy, escalators are now meaningfully embedded across the portfolio, and development yields are attractive relative to the cost of capital. These are all positive fundamentals to me.

The broader industrial environment also appears to be slowly turning back in landlords’ favor as new supply tapers and demand remains steady from 3PL, retail, and manufacturing users.

Moreover, the FR management team is guiding to another year of solid NOI and FFO/share growth. This is what I would expect to see from an industrial REIT at this point in the cycle.

At about 19x forward FFO, however, and trading near the top end of its 52-week range, a lot of this good news already feels reflected in the stock.

While there may be incremental upside if governance concerns raised by Land & Buildings gain traction and valuation gaps versus peers begin to close, that may take some time to flesh out, and I’m comfortable waiting for that to actually happen.

For now, FR looks like a solid industrial trading at a fair price in a market where selectivity still matters, in my view.

data.title: TSMC: Bulletproof Your Portfolio Even Though Peace Should Prevail
data.description:
It's that time in the evolution of mankind again where the governmental leaders are flaunting their guns and ships and trying to gamble their way to more and more market share on the global stage. For normal people, like you and I, we don't want to be involved, and we also want to maintain somewhat of a decent quality of life. Given you're reading this, you probably also have a portfolio. So, it only makes sense to begin thinking laterally.

That doesn't mean being fearful, but it does mean being shrewd. It's time to risk-adjust, and the main company in focus on the world stage is in Taiwan. You guessed it: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited, aka TSMC (TSM). Since my last analysis, the stock is up by 23% in price.

I don't really think Taiwan is going to be invaded or blockaded by China, but then again, I also haven't expected many things to happen in my life, and they still have. The purpose of this analysis isn't to impress you. I'm a portfolio trigger-puller, and so my research explains my thoughts, risk process, and methodology for stock selection and sizing. I want to make cash and live well. Therefore, the purpose of this analysis is to help you make simple but powerful portfolio moves that will protect you when other "geniuses" are getting burned. Keep it simple. If you can fry an egg, you can beat the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ).

You're Looking at Iran, While I'm Looking at Taiwan

Firstly, I want to look into some of the positives, because analyzing tail risk as a full analysis is a bit like being paranoid... I don't want to live in fear like that. I prefer to analyze the heavy probabilities and then spend a small amount of time on the "worst case," but have the countermeasures in place to quickly win in the case of any number of tail risks emerging.

Plausibly, we could see Arizona, Japan, and Germany scale TSMC fabs fast and furiously if Taiwan appeared to come under hard pressure. In fact, one has to wonder: if it is very important for China to reunify Taiwan with its mainland, then TSMC could at least be scaled abroad predominantly first. But I know there are other political questions that deserve to be answered, like who asked the Taiwanese what they want? That is another line of thought for another time.

In 2025, 75% of revenue came from North America-based customers and 58% from HPC. Already, the Atlantic Council and Rhodium have found that some G7 partners see quarantine and telecom disruption as viable and potential triggers for economic countermeasures. Pressure on TSMC would internationalize fast, even below war. It's logical, then, that markets at this valuation should not be viewed as geopolitically neutral (we've seen why with Hormuz; is this practice?! Only God knows. But no, not for the people of Iran).

There is an underpriced kill shot to be aware of, which is what RAND calls Taiwan's "Achilles heel." Taiwan's subsea cables could take weeks to repair if struck. All visible friendly satellite capacity is less than one modern cable before jamming. Of course, don't forget that TSMC itself says customer service depends on design, engineering, and logistics data flows, meaning that fabs can still be standing, but the operating system can be failing.

Okay, okay, let's look at the "worst-case" for all you doomers out there. I don't know why anyone would want this to happen or even be predicting it would, but some hawks say a Taiwan invasion is somewhat inevitable. So let's just imagine it happens. Firstly, my 37% cash and equivalents position would rise as the market falls. So I may be 60-70% in cash and equivalents in a depression low... I'd analyze so heavily and buy so much value I'd come out on top. I don't want that, but I'm just saying. And that's part of why I called my company "Invictus Origin," meaning "Unconquerable Essence."

DoD said a PRC Joint Blockade Campaign can already pair import cutoffs with missile strikes, offshore-island seizures, electronic warfare, cyber, and information operations for weeks or months. If Beijing were to then escalate to landings, TSMC would stop being a cash machine, and it would instead convert to an asset priced for survivability, not EPS.

TSMC's Valuation Is Sky-High

This environment is ludicrous in many ways to invest in. There are so many geopolitical variables that are out of portfolio managers' control that you have to just take a step back and be humble. Engineer portfolios to survive anything, and then you will thrive no matter what occurs.

Moving averages are always a good valuation and sentiment barometer, because over time studying markets, you begin to realize that stocks don't get priced fully relative to peers. Instead, some stocks take on a life of their own. Then, you need to find out what its relative value is compared to its own history. The caveat to this is that you need to be aware if a stock is ripping due to enormous forward fundamental growth.

With TSMC, we just need to be aware that growth is on a tear, but it's not pre-exponential. We're nearing the phase-down part of this growth curve. Consensus has normalized EPS returns at +36% for FY26 and +23.5% for FY27. Can growth rates stay higher for longer? Yes, but we also need to factor in valuation multiple compression now that growth rates are slowing. This is the point of diminishing returns. This is why I am somewhat skeptical of the present valuation.

In terms of a price target, here is my math, which is barring a Taiwan invasion (in which TSM stock would be obliterated) or a Taiwan containment operation (in which TSM stock would severely correct). I forecast April 2028 normalized EPS of $18.50, with a forward P/E in April 2027 of 20. This is the bull case and reflects 9% upside. Unfortunately, if growth slows heavily in 2028, then the forward P/E could become as low as 15 even with April 2028 normalized EPS of $18 or higher. This bear case reflects 18.5% downside. Therefore, you can see why my rating is Hold.

Conclusion: Hold

I'm a cautiously optimistic permabull with a knack for thriving in bear markets. In other words, don't underestimate my portfolio architecture! From the last tariff trough to today, my consolidated portfolio achieved a +75% return. Here's the evidence:

I'm not super bearish on TSMC stock, but I'm also shrewd enough to know that at this valuation, most of the alpha has already been attained and the goodness of the company is priced in. In essence, I want to buy pre-inflection and attain monster growth while also holding a meaty cash position to protect me from any negativity that may occur in the markets. I also don't want to spend my days trying to predict the impossible, and 30% cash or more in high valuation regimes lets me sleep well at night and keep taking convex bets in peace. Whatever happens, I'll keep winning and spreading positivity while I do.
